topic: My toll transponder shows forty seven crossings on a bridge in a state I have never driven through and the license plate photos on each crossing are my exact car
title: My Toll Transponder Has Forty Seven Crossings on a Bridge I Have Never Driven. The Photos Are My Car. | True Scary Story
description_intro: My E-ZPass account shows forty seven crossings on the Whitestone Causeway in eastern Maryland over the past sixteen months. I have never driven in Maryland. The transponder ID on each crossing matches the unit on my windshield. The license plate photographs match my exact car. Each crossing happened at three fourteen in the morning. Every other Tuesday.
description:
My E-ZPass account shows forty seven crossings on the Whitestone Causeway in eastern Maryland over the past sixteen months. I have never driven in Maryland. The transponder ID on each crossing matches the unit on my windshield. The license plate photographs match my exact car. Each crossing happened at three fourteen in the morning. Every other Tuesday.

The toll authority charges my linked debit card seven dollars and twenty five cents per crossing. I had not noticed the line items because they were rolled into a monthly autopay statement I never read. The amount works out to twenty nine dollars a month. The customer service representative I called told me my transponder had been activated by a second EZ Pass account opened under my exact name and address eighteen months ago. The second account was opened in person with a notarized signature. I have never been to the toll office.

There is a detail in the license plate photograph from the most recent crossing that I have only told my brother about. There is a passenger in the front seat. The passenger is also me. Watch to the end. There is another crossing scheduled tonight at three fourteen.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I opened my E-ZPass account online to dispute a charge on a bridge near my office. While I was logged in I scrolled through the full crossing history out of curiosity. The list went back sixteen months. Most of the entries were familiar. The interstate spur on my morning commute. The harbor bridge on the way to my mother's house. The toll plaza on the way to the airport when we flew at Christmas. There was also a second set of crossings I did not recognize. Forty seven entries on something called the Whitestone Causeway. The Whitestone Causeway is in eastern Maryland. I have never driven in Maryland. The forty seven crossings were spread across sixteen months at remarkably even intervals. Every other Tuesday. Three fourteen in the morning. Every time.

sections.narration: The Whitestone Causeway is a two and a half mile bridge across an inlet of Chesapeake Bay between two small towns on the eastern shore. I looked it up after I found the charges. It carries a state highway across the water. It opened in nineteen sixty four. The toll is collected electronically in both directions. There is one set of license plate cameras at each end. The nearest major city is forty miles south. The two towns the bridge connects are small enough that the entire eastern town has one stoplight and a gas station. The causeway is six hundred and eighty miles from my house. The drive would take eleven hours each way. I have never been to that part of Maryland. I have never crossed the Chesapeake Bay in any direction.

sections.narration: I have driven the same commute for nine years. Twenty two miles each way. Three toll plazas. The transponder on my windshield has been the same unit since I leased my current car four years ago. I read my E-ZPass statement once a quarter when I balance my budget. The total charges have always matched my expectations within a few dollars. I never scrolled below the first page of the crossing history. The Maryland charges had been rolled into the monthly autopay debit and absorbed into the totals without flagging anything. Twenty nine dollars a month. Across sixteen months. Four hundred and sixty four dollars in unexplained tolls. My wife had not noticed either. She uses our linked card only for groceries. The line items would have looked like commute expenses to anyone glancing.

sections.narration: Each crossing on the E-ZPass website has a small icon next to it. Clicking the icon brings up the license plate photograph captured by the toll camera. The photograph is small but readable. I opened the most recent Maryland entry. The license plate in the photograph was mine. The plate matched character for character. The car behind the plate was the same make, model, and color as my own. There was a small dent on the rear bumper that I had put there backing into a recycling bin two summers ago. I had never repaired the dent. The crossing time was three fourteen in the morning. I had been asleep in my own bed beside my wife at three fourteen that morning. My car had been in my own garage with the doors closed.

sections.narration: I closed the laptop. I told myself three things. One. The license plate digits on Maryland plates and on my home state plates were close enough that a low resolution camera could misread one for the other. Two. There were thousands of sedans in my exact make and color, and bumper dents were common. Three. The cluster of charges was probably an automated billing error between two toll authorities that share a clearinghouse. I have read about cases where rental car transponders cross billing systems and rack up phantom tolls. I emailed E-ZPass customer service at midnight. I asked them to investigate the forty seven crossings as fraudulent. I told myself I would have an apology email by morning. I went to bed. I did not sleep. The next morning the inbox had a response from customer service.

sections.narration: The customer service email told me there was no error. The Maryland crossings were not fraudulent. The crossings had been billed to a second E-ZPass account opened under my exact name and address eighteen months ago. The second account had been linked to my existing transponder ID at the time of opening. Both accounts had been autopaying tolls from the same linked debit card. The representative told me the second account had been opened in person at the regional toll office in our state, with a notarized signature, a copy of a valid driver's license, and a printed lease agreement matching my vehicle. The notary's name was on file. The signature on file looked correct to her. She forwarded me a scanned copy. The signature was mine. I have never been to the regional toll office.

sections.narration: I drove to the regional toll office on Friday morning. I asked the woman at the counter to pull the file for the second account. She did. I asked for the contact information for the notary listed on the form. She gave me a small business card. The notary was a woman who ran a mailbox and shipping store in a strip mall twelve miles from my house. I drove there immediately. The shop owner was named Mary. She remembered the appointment. She told me a man matching my description had come in with the documents. He had presented a valid driver's license that matched the photocopy in the file. He had signed in front of her. She had stamped and dated the document. She remembered him because he had paid the notary fee in folded one dollar bills.

sections.narration: I drove home and pulled up every Maryland crossing photograph in the account history. I downloaded all forty seven. I laid them out on my dining room table in chronological order. The car was the same in every photograph. Same make. Same color. Same dent. The license plate was mine in every photograph. The crossings were on the eastward direction in every photograph. The transponder had never logged a westward return crossing in sixteen months. If the car had crossed the causeway forty seven times eastbound and never westbound, it should be on the eastern shore right now. The photographs were stamped three fourteen in the morning. Every photograph. The light in each one was the same. The same shade of darkness across the water. The same star pattern in the sky above the bridge.

sections.narration: I picked up the most recent photograph and held it under the table lamp. I had not noticed it on the screen. The angle of the bridge camera caught the rear of the car but also a portion of the rear passenger window. The interior was visible through the window. There were two figures in the front of the car. The driver was facing forward. The passenger was turned toward the camera, looking out the back window. The passenger's face was sharp. The features were clear. The passenger was me. The driver had short brown hair, the same brand of work jacket I wear, and was wearing my watch on his left wrist. The watch face was visible in the photograph. The hour and minute hands read three fourteen. I have not removed that watch in nine years.

sections.narration: I closed the second E-ZPass account through the customer service phone line on Monday. The representative told me the account had already been closed twenty minutes before my call by the account holder. She told me the closing call had been made from my own home number. I had been at work at that time. The Maryland crossings stopped for two weeks. Then a new charge appeared on my linked card. A new account had been opened at a different regional toll office in a different state. The first crossing on the new account is scheduled for tonight at three fourteen in the morning, on a bridge I have never been to, in a state I have never driven through. The license plate in the file photo on the account is mine. My car is in the garage. The garage door is closed.
